reason: Closed-book solvability fails for at least one Correct-labeled option. Option C asserts that 0.66°C/s is near a thermal-runaway onset threshold and concludes this rate alone is not sufficient; the stem does not provide any threshold definition or typical onset criterion, so “near the threshold commonly associated” cannot be derived without paper-specific knowledge. Option A also relies on an unstated causal mechanism (“insufficient stored energy”) not logically implied by the given observations alone. Hence the item depends on hidden literature information.

MCQ with papers provided. The stem includes all needed numerical data (HRR, venting rate, FED) to judge A, B, and E via basic ratio/relative-change reasoning without paper-only definitions. Incorrect options C, D, and F are not defensible from the stem alone (they add unsupported scope/causality/safety generalizations). No correct-labeled statement contradicts the evidence bundle.

MCQ; paper evidence provided. The stem contains all needed quantitative trends (FED and venting rate vs SOC) and the key observation (no violent venting at 25% SOC). Options B and D are defensible using general engineering reasoning about scope/transferability: open-condition, cell-level FED does not guarantee safety in enclosed/poorly ventilated environments, and mechanical venting hazard reduction can be argued separately from asphyxiant exposure which depends on enclosure/ventilation. No Correct-labeled option contradicts the evidence; Incorrect options rely on overgeneralization (e.g., FED<1 implying universal safety; no violent venting implying no toxic gases), making the item non-ambiguous and closed-book solvable.

Short-answer item; paper evidence provided. The stem contains all needed numerical trends (pressure, HRR with/without secondary ignition, FED) and explicitly states the 25% vs 100% SOC comparisons, so the main conclusions and limitations can be derived via general engineering reasoning about relative hazard contributions and scope of cell-level/open-condition measurements. The gold answer’s key claims (monotonic/nonlinear pressure increase with SOC, >10× HRR increase with secondary ignition, FED increasing but low in absolute magnitude, 25% SOC not reaching violent thermal runaway, and limits on extrapolating to modules/enclosures) are consistent with and not contradicted by the evidence bundle, and do not require paper-only definitions beyond what is already in the stem.
